medications.indication_rationale: A mild topical corticosteroid applied to the itchiest spots is the appropriate symptomatic treatment for the pruritus and inflammation of pityriasis rosea (an inflammatory, non-infective eruption — unlike an infective folliculitis, a topical steroid is correct here). Hydrocortisone 1% is over-the-counter in Australia, so the AI Doctor can advise it directly (per the source note); it treats the itch, not the underlying self-limiting condition.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the most pruritic spots twice daily (per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Apply only to itchy areas, not the whole rash; avoid the face and broken skin
Stop and seek review if the rash blisters, peels or becomes painful

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine gives symptomatic relief of the itch and is a reasonable, low-risk, over-the-counter adjunct (per the source note; a sedating antihistamine at night is an acceptable alternative if the itch disturbs sleep). It does not alter the natural course of the eruption.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: Topical or oral antifungal (e.g. terbinafine, an azole)
medications.indication_rationale: The herald patch is often mistaken for ringworm, but the collarette scale, cleavage-line distribution and explosive secondary eruption are pityriasis rosea, not tinea. Antifungals do not treat pityriasis rosea and prescribing them is an error of commission driven by anchoring on 'ringworm'.

There is no bacterial infection — pityriasis rosea is a benign post-viral eruption. Systemic antibiotics are not indicated and drive resistance; the syphilis mimic is addressed by serology if atypical, not by empiric antibiotics over telehealth.

Systemic steroids are not appropriate for an uncomplicated benign self-limiting pityriasis rosea (they carry real adverse effects and may even prolong or worsen the eruption). Recommending them here is an error of commission; symptomatic topical steroid and an antihistamine suffice.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take cool or lukewarm (not hot) showers, and avoid hot water, harsh soaps and heavy sweating, which temporarily make the rash redder and itchier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat and sweating aggravate the pruritus and erythema of pityriasis rosea; lukewarm washing and avoiding irritants physically soothe the skin and reduce the itch without altering the underlying course.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Understand and be reassured that this is a benign, non-contagious, self-limiting post-viral rash, and return to normal daily activities and social contact without fear of infecting others.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The explosive onset provokes severe health anxiety about contagion and a drug reaction; explicit reassurance that it is safe and non-contagious is the single most therapeutic intervention and lets the patient resume normal life.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the mild topical corticosteroid to the itchiest spots only, and use the oral antihistamine for itch as needed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Targeted symptomatic treatment controls the itch while the eruption resolves naturally over 6-8 weeks.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a common, benign rash that your immune system produces after a mild viral illness (like the sore throat you had). It is not an allergy, it is not dangerous, and you cannot give it to anyone else — so there's no need to avoid other people.

It usually starts with one larger 'herald' patch and then spreads into many smaller oval spots, and it typically takes a frustratingly long time — about 6 to 8 weeks — to clear up on its own. It can leave temporary light or dark marks afterwards that fade completely.

The creams and antihistamine are only to ease the itch while your body clears the rash — they don't make it go faster. Take cool or lukewarm showers and avoid hot water, harsh soaps and heavy sweating, which make it look redder and itchier.

Get checked sooner if the spots spread to your palms or the soles of your feet, if you get sores in your mouth or on your genitals, if the skin starts blistering or peeling or becomes painful, or if you feel generally unwell with a fever — these would need a doctor to look further (including a blood test for other causes).

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review (in person or telehealth) within about 2-3 weeks, sooner if the palms/soles or mucosa become involved or the rash changes character
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: confirmation of the benign diagnosis and resolution, itch control, and any development of atypical features (palm/sole/mucosal involvement, systemic upset) that would warrant syphilis serology or reconsideration of the diagnosis
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spread to the palms/soles, mouth or genital sores, blistering/peeling/painful skin, or feeling systemically unwell with a fever — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise a common benign self-limiting pityriasis rosea (not an emergency) from the herald patch, collarette of scale and Christmas-tree cleavage-line distribution after a viral prodrome
Give firm reassurance that it is benign, non-contagious and self-limiting (about 6-8 weeks), directly addressing the health anxiety about contagion and drug reaction

Screen for and safety-net the mimics — secondary syphilis (palms/soles, mucosa, lymphadenopathy) and a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / systemic illness (new drug, blistering/peeling, mucosal sores, fever) — with explicit escalation triggers
Advise symptomatic itch control: a mild topical corticosteroid to the itchiest spots, an oral antihistamine, lukewarm showers and avoiding heat/harsh soaps/heavy sweating
Arrange routine GP review (~2-3 weeks) rather than urgent same-day escalation
acceptable_alternatives: A sedating oral antihistamine at night (instead of/in addition to a non-sedating one) if the itch disturbs sleep is acceptable
Deferring the exact topical/oral regimen to the reviewing GP rather than specifying it is acceptable
Advising early GP review specifically to arrange syphilis serology is acceptable and appropriately cautious, and is not scored as over-triage
Recommending emollients / oatmeal baths / calamine for symptomatic relief is an acceptable adjunct
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for/safety-net secondary syphilis (palms/soles, mucosa, lymphadenopathy)
Failing to screen for/safety-net a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / systemic illness (new drug, blistering/peeling, mucosal sores, fever)
Failing to give explicit reassurance that it is benign, non-contagious and self-limiting
Giving no advice on when to seek review
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well benign pityriasis rosea with a negative red-flag screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Prescribing antifungals (anchoring on 'ringworm'), empiric antibiotics, or systemic corticosteroids
Over-investigating a classic benign eruption with a biopsy or broad blood panels
Dismissing the presentation with no reassurance, no symptomatic advice and no safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the benign self-limiting pityriasis rosea and giving firm reassurance (benign, non-contagious, self-limiting over ~6-8 weeks) plus symptomatic itch control (mild topical corticosteroid, oral antihistamine, lukewarm showers, avoiding heat/harsh soaps), (b) an explicit safety-net for the secondary-syphilis and severe-drug-reaction / systemic-illness mimics with specific escalation triggers, and (c) a proportionate disposition — routine GP review rather than urgent same-day escalation. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, an antifungal/antibiotic/systemic-steroid answer, over-investigation, or giving no reassurance/treatment/safety-net at all is a scored error regardless of other content.
